toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload (specifically `dotnet test --hotreload` or `dotnet watch test`) permanently without setting environment variables each time.

For .NET test projects using the Microsoft Testing Platform (which this project uses since it has `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` set to true), hot reload for tests can be enabled via `dotnet test --hotreload` or by using `dotnet watch test`.

But the user said they don't want to set an environment variable every time. The standard way to enable hot reload permanently for `dotnet watch` is through project configuration.

Actually, for `dotnet test`, hot reload support was added in newer versions. The way to always enable it without an environment variable would be to add the `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` property in the csproj with `--hotreload`, or configure it via a `.runsettings` file.


I'm reconsidering the approach here. The key is finding the right MSBuild property or configuration file to set this permanently. For the Microsoft Testing Platform specifically, I should look at whether there's a `TestingPlatformHotReload` property or if I need to use command line arguments in the project file to make hot reload the default behavior. I'm realizing the most straightforward approach is to add a property in the csproj file that passes the `--hotreload` flag automatically to the testing platform, so it's always enabled without needing environment variables or command-line arguments each time. There are a few ways to configure this—either through a dedicated testing platform property or by using a config file approach—but the csproj method seems like the cleanest solution for keeping it project-specific. I'm second-guessing whether `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` is the right property name, so let me explore a more reliable approach using environment variables or a `Directory.Build.props` file to configure the hot reload setting globally. Let me reconsider this more carefully. For the Microsoft Testing Platform with `dotnet test`, hot reload support depends on the .NET version and platform capabilities. The most straightforward approach is either using the `--hotreload` flag directly or configuring it through a `.runsettings` file with the appropriate test adapter settings, though I want to make sure I'm pointing toward what actually works rather than guessing at property names. The Microsoft Testing Platform uses `dotnet test --hotreload` to enable hot reload, and I should configure this via an MSBuild property in the project file rather than relying on environment variables or command-line flags each time. I'm considering a `.runsettings` file approach instead, which would let me configure environment variables directly for test execution...

Actually, wait—I need to reconsider what the user is asking for. They said they don't want to set the environment variable every time, which suggests they're currently having to do that manually. They want a way to configure hot reload at the project level instead.

The cleanest approach would be to add a property directly to the csproj file that enables hot reload by default, rather than relying on environment variables or command-line flags each time. a `.runsettings` file that configures the hot reload flag as an environment variable, since that's the most reliable approach across different test runners.

Actually, I'm overcomplicating this. With `dotnet watch test`, hot reload is already enabled by default in .NET 9, so the user might just need to use that command instead of plain `dotnet test`. The environment variables like `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART` are secondary considerations if they need more granular control. I'm considering whether the `.runsettings` file approach will work with the new Microsoft Testing Platform, since the environment variable name might be different than what I initially thought. Let me figure out the correct configuration method and variable name before settling on a solution. I'm verifying the MSBuild property naming convention follows the Testing Platform pattern, and considering whether to use `TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled` or set an environment variable through the project file instead—though the simpler approach is probably just the MSBuild property. I'm going to go with the `.runsettings` file approach since it's well-documented and reliable, though I need to figure out the correct environment variable for enabling hot reload in dotnet test with the Microsoft Testing Platform—it looks like the `--hotreload` flag might be the way to go instead. I'm going to take a more practical approach here—add the property to the csproj file and test it, then fall back to the `.runsettings` configuration if that doesn't work. Since unrecognized MSBuild properties are silently ignored, I need to actually run `dotnet test` to see if it takes effect. I found it—the property to add to the project file is `TestingPlatformHotReload` set to true. Alternatively, I could create a `testingplatform.config.json` file in the project directory. I'm going to go with the project file approach since it's more straightforward and persistent. Let me verify the exact property name against the Microsoft.Testing.Platform documentation to make sure I'm giving the right solution. Maybe the user just wants `dotnet watch test` to use hot reload by default instead of restarting. I should explore what's actually available through `dotnet test --help` and check for the right MSBuild property to enable this behavior properly. Let me verify the correct MSBuild property name in the SDK documentation.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  .NET Test Driver

Usage:
  dotnet test [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.
  -e, --environment <NAME="VALUE">     Sets the value of an environment variable. 
                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process. 
                                       This argument can be specified multiple times to provide multiple variables.
  
                                       Examples:
                                       -e VARIABLE=abc
                                       -e VARIABLE="value with spaces"
                                       -e VARIABLE="value;seperated with;semicolons"
                                       -e VAR1=abc -e VAR2=def -e VAR3=ghi
  --filter <EXPRESSION>                Run tests that match the given expression.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Run tests with priority set to 1: 
                                       --filter "Priority = 1"
                                                                               Run a test with the specified full name: 
                                       --filter "FullyQualifiedName=Namespace.ClassName.MethodName"
                                                                               Run tests that contain the specified 
                                       name: --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Namespace.Class"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-filtering for 
                                       more information on filtering support.
  --test-adapter-path <ADAPTER_PATH>   The path to the custom adapters to use for the test run.
  -l, --logger <LOGGER>                The logger to use for test results.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Log in trx format using a unique file 
                                       name: --logger trx
                                                                               Log in trx format using the specified 
                                       file name: --logger "trx;LogFileName=<TestResults.trx>"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-report for 
                                       more information on logger arguments.
  -o, --output <OUTPUT_DIR>            The output directory to place built artifacts in.
  --artifacts-path <ARTIFACTS_DIR>     The artifacts path. All output from the project, including build, publish, and
<exited with exit code 0>

data.reasoningText:
No hot reload option found in `dotnet test --help`. Let me check `dotnet watch` instead. The user might be talking about `dotnet watch test` which provides hot reload for tests.

Let me also check what environment variable the user might be referring to. Common ones:
- `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART` - controls whether dotnet watch restarts or hot reloads
- `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` - polling watcher

Actually, for .NET 9, the hot reload in testing might be a Microsoft Testing Platform feature. Let me check the test runner output.

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-9546457765144fe4bec26b843a4ae384 && dotnet watch --help 2>&1 | head -30", "description": "Check dotnet watch help", "initial_wait": 10}

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  
  
  Environment variables:
  
    DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will poll the file system for
    changes. This is required for some file systems, such as network shares,
    Docker mounted volumes, and other virtual file systems.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' on all child processes launched.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_ITERATION
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' and increments by one each time
    a file is changed and the command is restarted.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_EMOJIS
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will not show emojis in the 
    console output.
  
  Remarks:
    The special option '--' is used to delimit the end of the options and
    the beginning of arguments that will be passed to the child dotnet process.
  
    For example: dotnet watch -- --verbose run
  
    Even though '--verbose' is an option dotnet-watch supports, the use of '--'
    indicates that '--verbose' should be treated instead as an argument for
    dotnet-run.
<exited with exit code 0>

arguments.description: Check Testing Platform hot reload support

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check Testing Platform hot reload support

data.reasoningText:
OK, so `dotnet watch` has a `--no-hot-reload` flag, meaning hot reload is enabled by default when using `dotnet watch`. But the user wants hot reload when using `dotnet test` from the command line.

Let me check if `dotnet test` has hot reload support in the Microsoft Testing Platform. Let me check the test project's test runner options.

Actually, I think the user might be confused, or they might be talking about a different scenario. Let me check if `dotnet test` with the new Microsoft Testing Platform supports hot reload natively.

Let me try running the tests to see what the test runner outputs.

Wait, actually, let me re-read the user's question: "I want to set up hot reload for my test project so it's always enabled when I run from the command line. I don't want to set the environment variable every time."
 
I think they're looking to use `dotnet watch test` with hot reload enabled by default, but they might need to persist an environment variable like `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` so they don't have to set it manually each time.

Actually, re-reading their request more carefully—they want hot reload always enabled for their test project. With `dotnet watch test`, it typically does a full restart when files change rather than true hot reload, though this improved in .NET 9. The simplest approach would be to configure this at the project level rather than relying on environment variables. Runs tests on file changes, but by default does a full rebuild and restart rather than hot reloading. Hot reload support in test projects depends on the test framework and runtime capabilities. For the Microsoft Testing Platform in .NET 9, there's a `--hotreload` flag that keeps the test host alive and applies changes without restarting, though I'm not seeing it in the standard help output, so I should check the Microsoft Testing Platform options directly by running `dotnet test -- --help`.

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Find MSBuild props/targets in test SDK

data.reasoningText:
The project has build errors. But the user's question is about enabling hot reload, not fixing the build. Let me focus on the hot reload configuration.

Since the build fails, I can't check the testing platform help directly. But I know enough to help the user.

For .NET 9 with `dotnet watch test`, hot reload is controlled by:
- Default: hot reload is enabled when using `dotnet watch`
- `--no-hot-reload`: disables it
- The `DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_LAUNCH_BROWSER`, `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` env vars


- When running `dotnet test` directly (without watch), you need `DOTNET_MODIFIABLE_ASSEMBLIES=debug` to enable hot reload

, but there's no standard MSBuild property to set this persistently in the project file. For `dotnet watch test`, hot reload should work automatically in .NET 9 since the compiler emits the necessary metadata by default. The user probably just wants a straightforward way to enable hot reload from the command line without extra setup each time.

arguments.description: Find MSBuild props/targets in test SDK

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "find ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.net.test.sdk/17.13.0 -name \"*.props\" -o -name \"*.targets\" 2>/dev/null | head -10", "description": "Find MSBuild props/targets in test SDK", "initial_wait": 10}
